text.title: Value Analysis and Pricing Models
text.summary: How to calculate cost floor, measure customer value ceiling, use Van Westendorp surveys to find acceptable price range, and select the right pricing model.

rule.statement: Set price at 10–20% of the measurable value delivered to customers. If your product saves $500/month, charge $50–100/month.
rule.rationale: Cost-plus pricing ignores value and systematically underprices. Competitor-copying ignores differentiation. Value-based pricing anchors the conversation to ROI, which is why customers buy.

section.title: Cost Floor Calculation
section.p: Sum fixed costs (hosting, tools, salaries), variable costs per customer (support time, API calls), and opportunity cost (your time at hourly rate x hours). This floor is the minimum viable price — below it, the business loses money.

section.title: Customer Value Ceiling
section.p: Quantify value across four types: time saved (hours/month x customer's hourly rate), money saved (cost reduction vs. alternatives), money earned (revenue increase enabled), and risk reduced (cost of problems prevented). The ceiling is the maximum a rational customer would pay.

rule.statement: Ask these four questions to find the acceptable price range: (1) Too expensive? (2) Expensive but worth considering? (3) A bargain? (4) So cheap it raises quality concerns? The optimal price is the intersection of "too cheap" and "expensive" responses.
rule.rationale: Van Westendorp avoids anchoring bias (you don't suggest price points) and surfaces both the floor and ceiling from the buyer's perspective. Minimum sample: 30 target customers.

list.item: Flat rate — simple products with uniform value delivery. One price, all features.
Tiered (Good/Better/Best) — feature differentiation exists; anchors the middle tier as the obvious choice.
Usage-based — value delivery is variable (API calls, seats, volume). Pay-per-unit aligns cost with value received.
Per-seat — team tools where value scales with users. Price per user per month.
Freemium — high volume, low-touch; free tier generates leads, paid converts them.
Hybrid (base + usage) — SaaS with variable infrastructure costs; base covers fixed, usage covers variable.
